title: Billy Fox (boxer)

infobox.value: William McKinley Fox (1926-02-01)February 1, 1926  [Tatums, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tatums,_Oklahoma "Tatums, Oklahoma"), U.S.
January 1986(1986-01-00) (aged 59)  [New York City, New York](/wiki/New_York_City,_New_York "New York City, New York"), U.S.

(February 1, 1926 – January 1986), better known as **"Blackjack" Billy Fox**, was an American [light heavyweight](/wiki/Light_heavyweight "Light heavyweight") boxer who is best known for having won a controversial fight against future [middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_middleweight_boxing_champions "List of middleweight boxing champions") [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta").[[1]](#cite_note-SI-1)

[*Professional Boxing: Hearings Before the United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary, Subcommittee on Antitrust and Monopoly, Eighty-Sixth Congress, Second Session, Eighty-Seventh Congress, First Session, Eighty-Eighth Congress, Second Session, Parts 1–4*](https://books.google.com/books?id=pGRFAQAAMAAJ&dq=%22william+mckinley+fox%22&pg=PA41). U.S. Government Printing Office. 1960. p. 41. Retrieved 5 November 2022.
["Mrs. Beulah Fox"](https://www.newspapers.com/clip/112568708/mrs-beulah-fox/). Richmond Times-Dispatch. 7 January 1933. p. 4. Retrieved 5 November 2022.

["BLINKY PALERMO, MOBSTER WHO RAN BOXING DIES!"](https://web.archive.org/web/20120225041904/http://ringtalk.com/blinky-palermo-mobster-who-ran-boxing-dies). Ring Talk. Archived from [the original](http://ringtalk.com/blinky-palermo-mobster-who-ran-boxing-dies) on 25 February 2012. Retrieved 9 September 2011.
["Most Consecutive Knockouts in the History of Boxing!"](https://www.boxingnews24.com/2022/08/most-consecutive-knockouts-in-the-history-of-boxing/). 4 August 2022.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American boxer (1926–1986)

For other people named Billy Fox, see [Billy Fox (disambiguation)](/wiki/Billy_Fox_(disambiguation) "Billy Fox (disambiguation)").

Billy Fox

**William McKinley Fox** (February 1, 1926 – January 1986), better known as **"Blackjack" Billy Fox**, was an American [light heavyweight](/wiki/Light_heavyweight "Light heavyweight") boxer who is best known for having won a controversial fight against future [middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_middleweight_boxing_champions "List of middleweight boxing champions") [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta").[[1]](#cite_note-SI-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Billy_Fox_(boxer)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

William McKinley Fox[[2]](#cite_note-2) was born in [Tatums, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tatums,_Oklahoma "Tatums, Oklahoma") on February 1, 1926,[[3]](#cite_note-WWII-3) to Melvin Calvin Fox (died 1984)[[4]](#cite_note-Calvin-4) and Beulah Carter (died 1933).[[5]](#cite_note-5) After his mother died when he was a child, he was raised by his stepmother, Viola Harris Fox.[[4]](#cite_note-Calvin-4)[[6]](#cite_note-obit-6)

Fox enlisted for service in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in 1944, when he was 18. At the time, he was resident in [Philadelphia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia, Pennsylvania").[[3]](#cite_note-WWII-3)

## Boxing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Billy_Fox_(boxer)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boxing career")]

Billy Fox, known as "Blackjack", started off his career by winning 36 consecutive fights and holds the best knockout ratio in boxing history going 36 wins with 36 knockouts before he was knocked out by [Gus Lesnevich](/wiki/Gus_Lesnevich "Gus Lesnevich") in ten rounds for the world light heavyweight title. Fox had the champion hurt in round three.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He would rack up 7 more wins, including a win in a bout thrown by [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta").[[8]](#cite_note-ReelLife-8) That fight was fixed by the notorious [Frank "Blinky" Palermo](/wiki/Frank_Palermo "Frank Palermo"), a member of the [Philadelphia crime family](/wiki/Philadelphia_crime_family "Philadelphia crime family"), who owned Fox under the table.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Fox then lost two fights in a row against Red Willis Applegate and [Gus Lesnevich](/wiki/Gus_Lesnevich "Gus Lesnevich"), to whom he lost in the first round by a knockout in 1:58 seconds, in a rematch that was also for the world light heavyweight title.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Fox would fight 12 more fights, and amass a record of 49 wins (48 by knockout), 9 losses, and 1 draw. His knockout streak of 42 fights is the second longest in boxing history.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Billy_Fox_(boxer)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Fox married Clara Rosemary Allen in [Dauphin, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Dauphin,_Pennsylvania "Dauphin, Pennsylvania"), on May 5, 1947.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) They met at the USO-Variety Club canteen whilst Fox was a soldier and Allen was a hostess.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1956, Fox was tracked down by *[Sports Illustrated](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated "Sports Illustrated")* journalists, at which point he was described as "living on the edges, desolate, vagrant, despairing". By July 1960, Fox was residing at a mental institution in [Long Island, New York](/wiki/Long_Island,_New_York "Long Island, New York"), and was said to be "seriously ill".[[1]](#cite_note-SI-1) He died in New York City in January 1986, at the age of 59.[[6]](#cite_note-obit-6)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Billy_Fox_(boxer)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

Billy Fox was portrayed in [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* by [Eddie Mustafa Muhammad](/wiki/Eddie_Mustafa_Muhammad "Eddie Mustafa Muhammad").
